headline: Oakland Firefighters Give Oxygen to Injured Pigeon
meta_description: After battling an East Oakland vehicle fire, firefighters administer oxygen to a distressed pigeon that walked up seeking help.
summary_short: Following a vehicle fire in East Oakland, firefighters from the E29 crew noticed a pigeon in distress and administered oxygen to help clear its lungs.
body:
A pigeon walked straight up to Oakland firefighters seeking help after suffering smoke inhalation during a vehicle fire on Wednesday evening. The E29 crew, who had just finished attending to the East Oakland blaze, noticed the bird in distress and sprang into action, administering oxygen to clear its lungs.

The viral video of the rescue reveals something deeper than a simple act of mercy. Many people assume pigeons are wild birds, but commenters on the Oakland Firefighters Local 55 Facebook post highlighted an often-overlooked truth: pigeons are domesticated animals, and they recognize humans as potential helpers.

"We domesticated pigeons—they come to us for help because we trained them to," wrote one commenter. Others echoed the sentiment, noting that these birds watch us closely and possess remarkable intelligence. "The trust that pigeon has for him… It knows the man is a life saver," observed another.

Within moments of receiving oxygen, the pigeon flew away, restored to health. The rescue captures something quietly profound about human-animal bonds—not the dramatic heroism of headlines, but the simple, mutual trust that forms between species who have lived alongside each other for centuries.
